company.name: Gilead Sciences
company.description: Gilead Sciences is a U.S. biopharmaceutical company focused on HIV, liver disease, oncology, and inflammatory disease medicines.

overview.title: Business position
paragraphs: Gilead's commercial base is anchored by HIV medicines, led by Biktarvy, with additional revenue from oncology, liver disease, COVID-19 antiviral products, and cell therapy.
The company reported $29.4 billion of 2025 revenue and $8.5 billion of net income, with first-quarter 2026 results showing continued HIV strength and growth for Trodelvy.

overview.title: Registry relevance
paragraphs: Gilead is a useful registry case because its moat is not distribution software or hardware lock-in; it is the regulated, patent-protected, evidence-heavy drug development stack around high-need disease categories.

Open science tooling can pressure discovery costs and target selection, but finished regulated medicines remain difficult to decentralize because manufacturing, clinical evidence, pharmacovigilance, payer access, and liability are tightly coupled.

moatNarrative: Gilead's moat is high because its leading products depend on patents, clinical trial evidence, regulatory approvals, prescriber trust, manufacturing quality systems, reimbursement access, and long-running disease-area expertise.

The HIV franchise is especially durable because once-daily antiretroviral regimens compete on efficacy, safety, resistance profile, adherence, and guideline acceptance rather than on ordinary product substitutability.
decentralizationNarrative: The parts of Gilead most exposed to decentralization are upstream research workflows: target discovery, molecular modeling, binding-energy calculation, public omics integration, and protocol sharing can all become more open and reproducible.

The finished-drug layer is much less decentralizable. Approved HIV and oncology medicines require validated chemistry, biologics or antibody-drug conjugate manufacturing, controlled clinical trials, regulatory submissions, safety monitoring, and payer channels that are not credibly replaced by a lightweight peer-to-peer network today.
company.snapshotNote: Queued from the May 25, 2026 S&P 500 market-cap snapshot ranks 51-75; market-cap and operating metrics refreshed on 2026-05-25.

moat.rationale: Patents, regulatory approvals, clinical evidence, prescribing relationships, manufacturing quality controls, and reimbursement access create a very strong moat around Gilead's major therapies.

decentralizability.rationale: Open discovery infrastructure can decentralize some upstream research, but approved pharmaceutical products remain constrained by clinical trials, regulatory approval, manufacturing validation, and pharmacovigilance.

profitability.rationale: Gilead reported $8.5 billion of 2025 net income on $29.4 billion of revenue, indicating strong profitability after the prior year's impairment-heavy result.

peRatio.rationale: Approximate trailing P/E calculated from CompaniesMarketCap's May 2026 market capitalization of $166.39 billion divided by Gilead's 2025 net income of $8.5 billion.

marketCap.rationale: CompaniesMarketCap listed Gilead Sciences at approximately $166.39 billion in market capitalization as of May 2026.

products.name: Biktarvy
products.summary: Biktarvy is Gilead's once-daily single-tablet regimen for treating HIV-1 in eligible adults and children.
products.whyItMatters: Biktarvy is a core driver of Gilead's HIV franchise and illustrates how a high-value pharmaceutical product combines medicinal chemistry, adherence design, clinical evidence, regulatory approval, and payer access.
replacementSketch: The realistic open alternative is not a community-made pill replacing Biktarvy directly. It is a more open discovery and evidence pipeline that improves target selection, resistance analysis, generic transition planning, and treatment-access coordination.

Over time, public datasets, open computational biology, and nonprofit manufacturing partnerships could reduce dependence on a single proprietary sponsor for the next generation of HIV regimens, while still requiring regulated manufacturers and clinical evidence.

alternatives.name: Open Targets Platform
alternatives.summary: Open Targets is a freely available, open-source target-identification and prioritization platform built from public biomedical datasets.

openness.rationale: The platform is freely available, provides data access and APIs, and publishes pipeline and infrastructure code as open source.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It decentralizes access to discovery intelligence, but it does not decentralize clinical trials, regulatory approval, or medicine manufacturing.

readiness.rationale: The platform is actively maintained, accessible through a user interface, API, and downloads, and is designed for practical target prioritization.

costLeverage.rationale: Open target evidence can reduce duplicated discovery work, though the expensive clinical and regulatory portions of HIV drug development remain.

alternatives.name: OpenFold
alternatives.summary: OpenFold is a free and open-source AI biology toolkit for protein-structure modeling and drug-discovery research.

openness.rationale: OpenFold publishes open-source software, available model weights, and a permissive training pipeline for academic and commercial use.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It broadens who can run advanced molecular modeling, but finished drug validation and manufacturing remain centralized and regulated.

readiness.rationale: The project is usable by research groups and companies, though it is a research platform rather than an approved therapeutic substitute.

costLeverage.rationale: Open protein modeling can lower barriers to early discovery and hypothesis generation before expensive wet-lab and clinical stages.

disruptionConcepts.name: Federated HIV discovery and access network
disruptionConcepts.summary: A federation of public labs, nonprofit manufacturers, clinicians, and patient groups could use open target evidence, open molecular modeling, and shared treatment-access data to accelerate follow-on HIV regimen research and coordinate access without depending entirely on one branded sponsor.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market structure shifts from a single sponsor controlling most discovery and commercialization decisions toward interoperable research and access nodes that can share evidence, candidates, and implementation lessons.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Federation matters more than Bitcoin here: the core advantage is shared, auditable biomedical evidence and multi-party coordination across research, manufacturing, and access programs.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Labs publish target and molecule evidence into shared repositories; clinicians and patient groups contribute access and adherence signals; nonprofit or generic manufacturers coordinate candidates that merit regulated development.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Evidence quality is constrained by public protocols, reproducible computational workflows, registered studies, peer review, adverse-event reporting, and ultimately regulator-reviewed clinical trials. The model cannot bypass drug approval.
failureModes: Open discovery may identify candidates without funding the expensive clinical trials needed for approval.
Data sharing can be biased, incomplete, or privacy-constrained, especially for patient-level adherence and resistance data.
Patent thickets or manufacturing know-how can still block practical substitution.
adoptionPath: Use open target and protein-modeling tools for resistance-aware HIV candidate discovery and repurposing screens.
Create nonprofit clinical-development partnerships around the most promising candidates or generic-transition opportunities.
Route validated therapies through regulated manufacturers and public-health procurement rather than informal distribution.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The concept decentralizes discovery and coordination but leaves approval and manufacturing in regulated institutional hands.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open biomedical platforms already support shared evidence, but aligning funders, trial sponsors, clinicians, and manufacturers is harder than sharing software.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: The upstream tooling exists, but the clinical, regulatory, and manufacturing path for an HIV medicine remains expensive and slow.

incumbentPressure.rationale: This could pressure discovery costs and access narratives, but it is unlikely to displace a leading approved regimen quickly.

products.name: Trodelvy
products.summary: Trodelvy is a Trop-2-directed antibody-drug conjugate used for certain metastatic breast cancer settings.
products.whyItMatters: Trodelvy shows the strongest version of a pharmaceutical moat: complex biologic targeting, cytotoxic payload delivery, clinical survival evidence, safety management, specialist prescribing, and regulated manufacturing.
replacementSketch: A direct open replacement for Trodelvy is not credible today because antibody-drug conjugates require specialized biologics engineering, linker-payload chemistry, validated manufacturing, and large clinical trials.

The plausible challenge is upstream and adjacent: open platforms can make target validation, biomarker selection, molecular simulation, and trial hypothesis generation more transparent and less dependent on proprietary pipelines.

alternatives.name: Open Free Energy
alternatives.summary: Open Free Energy is an open-source ecosystem for binding free-energy calculations used in pharmaceutical lead optimization.

openness.rationale: The project develops public GitHub software under a permissive MIT license.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It decentralizes access to computational lead optimization, but it does not replace biologics manufacturing, toxicology, or clinical validation.

readiness.rationale: The tools are designed for drug-discovery workflows and developed with industry feedback, though they are not therapeutic products.

costLeverage.rationale: Open lead-optimization tooling can reduce duplicated computational work, but Trodelvy-like antibody-drug conjugates still require expensive experimental and clinical programs.

alternatives.name: Open Targets Platform
alternatives.summary: Open Targets provides open-source infrastructure and public biomedical data for therapeutic target identification and prioritization.

openness.rationale: Open Targets makes data, APIs, downloads, and open-source code available for target-disease evidence work.

decentralizationFit.rationale: It opens target-prioritization workflows to more participants while leaving clinical development and manufacturing centralized.

readiness.rationale: The platform is active, documented, and structured for practical drug-discovery research.

costLeverage.rationale: Better shared target evidence can reduce dead-end discovery spending, but it does not remove the high cost of oncology trials.

disruptionConcepts.name: Open oncology ADC discovery commons
disruptionConcepts.summary: An open oncology discovery commons could pool target-disease evidence, tumor biomarker data, molecular modeling, and preclinical protocol outputs so more institutions can evaluate antibody-drug conjugate candidates before a proprietary sponsor controls the full pipeline.
disruptionConcepts.thesis: The market changes if early target validation and candidate prioritization become shared infrastructure, reducing the ability of a single incumbent to monopolize discovery bottlenecks before clinical development begins.
disruptionConcepts.bitcoinOrDecentralizationRole: Decentralization is the relevant mechanism: federated labs and data stewards coordinate around reproducible evidence rather than one closed R&D pipeline. Bitcoin is not central to the trust model.
disruptionConcepts.coordinationMechanism: Academic labs, hospitals, computational biology groups, and nonprofit sponsors contribute target evidence, biomarker cohorts, assay protocols, and negative results into a governed commons.
disruptionConcepts.verificationOrTrustModel: Participants rely on reproducible workflows, provenance-tagged datasets, registered preclinical protocols, independent replication, institutional review, and eventual regulator-reviewed trials. False claims are constrained by requiring raw evidence and replication before candidates advance.
failureModes: Sensitive oncology data can be hard to share because of privacy, consent, and competitive restrictions.
Open target evidence does not solve payload toxicity, linker design, biologics manufacturing, or trial execution.
A successful candidate may still be captured by exclusive financing or patent strategies.
adoptionPath: Start with open target and biomarker prioritization for Trop-2-adjacent or resistance-related oncology hypotheses.
Fund shared preclinical replication packages for the strongest candidates.
Move only validated candidates into regulated clinical development through nonprofit, public-private, or generic-oriented sponsors.

decentralizationFit.rationale: The commons can decentralize discovery evidence and prioritization, but approved oncology biologics remain institutionally manufactured and regulated.

coordinationCredibility.rationale: Open science platforms make shared evidence coordination plausible, but clinical oncology development requires larger governance and funding commitments.

implementationFeasibility.rationale: ADC development is technically difficult and capital intensive, so open tooling helps upstream discovery more than finished-product substitution.

incumbentPressure.rationale: The concept could improve the discovery ecosystem but is unlikely to pressure Trodelvy's approved-market position in the near term.

summaryMarkdown: Prepared a new Gilead Sciences bundle covering Biktarvy and Trodelvy, with market-cap, profitability, moat, and decentralization metrics refreshed from May 2026 sources. The strongest disruption framing is upstream open drug discovery and federated research coordination rather than direct decentralized manufacture of regulated medicines.
